title: Diffusing in Pixel Space Is Expensive
concept: Running hundreds of denoising steps directly on full-resolution images (e.g. 512x512 pixels) is computationally very expensive.

exercises.prompt: If you had to clean up a giant 512x512 pixel photo bit by bit, hundreds of times, how would that feel?

options.text: Fast and cheap - no big deal
Slow and expensive - tons of tiny dots to fix again and again

exercises.explain: A 512x512 image has over 260,000 pixels, and doing math on all of them, hundreds of times, adds up fast!

cards.title: Denoising Is Expensive!
cards.body: Imagine repainting every single dot on a giant poster, hundreds of times, just to slowly reveal a picture. That's what AI image generators do when they denoise directly in pixel space - and it costs a LOT of computer power and time.

cards.title: What Does 'Pixel Space' Mean?
cards.body: Pixel space just means working directly with every tiny colored dot (pixel) in an image. A 512x512 image has 262,144 pixels! Running hundreds of denoising steps on ALL of them, over and over, takes huge amounts of computing power and time.

cards.title: Cleaning a Stadium vs. a Model
cards.body: Denoising in pixel space is like cleaning every single seat in a giant stadium by hand, one at a time, hundreds of times. It would be way faster to first shrink the stadium down to a tiny model, clean THAT, then blow it back up to full size!

cards.title: Real Example
cards.body: Early diffusion models generated images by denoising raw pixels directly. For a 512x512 image, each of the ~260,000 pixels gets updated at every one of the hundreds of steps - meaning billions of tiny calculations just for one picture!

cards.title: Myth: Bigger Isn't Really a Problem
cards.body: Myth: Working with full-size images doesn't cost that much more than smaller ones. Truth: Pixel count grows FAST as images get bigger (double the size = 4x the pixels!), so full-resolution diffusion can become painfully slow and costly.

cards.title: Pixel Space vs. Compressed Space
cards.body: Doing denoising directly on full pixels versus doing it on a smaller, compressed version makes a huge difference in speed and cost.

left.text: Hundreds of steps on 260,000+ pixels = slow, expensive, heavy on hardware

right.text: Work on a small shrunk version first = much faster and cheaper (this is what later lessons will explore!)

exercises.prompt: Why is diffusing directly in pixel space so expensive?

options.text: Because it only takes one step to finish
Because hundreds of denoising steps run on every pixel in a full-resolution image

exercises.explain: A full-resolution image has huge numbers of pixels, and denoising runs on all of them across hundreds of steps - that's a massive amount of computation.

exercises.prompt: A 512x512 image has over 260,000 pixels that each need to be processed at every denoising step.
exercises.explain: 512 x 512 = 262,144 pixels - and each one is touched at every single denoising step, hundreds of times!

exercises.sentence: Running denoising directly on full-resolution images is computationally very ______ because of the huge number of pixels involved.
exercises.explain: It's 'expensive' because processing hundreds of steps across hundreds of thousands of pixels takes a lot of computing power and time.

exercises.prompt: What would happen if you doubled the width and height of an image before diffusing it in pixel space?

options.text: The pixel count would roughly quadruple, making it even more expensive to process
The pixel count would stay exactly the same
The pixel count would only double, keeping cost the same

exercises.explain: Doubling both width and height multiplies the total pixel count by about 4, since area grows with both dimensions - making pixel-space diffusion even more costly.

exercises.prompt: An AI team wants to generate 1024x1024 images using hundreds of denoising steps directly in pixel space. What's the main challenge they'll face?

options.text: The images will have no colors
It will require huge amounts of computing power and time due to the massive number of pixels processed at every step
The AI will refuse to generate images that large
Larger images always look worse than smaller ones

exercises.explain: A 1024x1024 image has over 1 million pixels. Processing all of them across hundreds of denoising steps directly in pixel space demands enormous computational resources - this is exactly why techniques like compressing images first (into 'latent space') were developed to make diffusion faster and cheaper.
